en.extract:
The Nechisar nightjar (Caprimulgus solala) is a species of nightjar in the family Caprimulgidae. It is now thought to be a hybrid. It is endemic to Ethiopia.
The species was first discovered in 1990 when researchers discovered a decomposing specimen in the Nechisar National Park. After bringing back a single wing from the specimen to the Natural History Museum in London, it was determined to be a previously unknown species. Its specific name, solala, means "only a wing".
Its natural habitat is subtropical. It is probably endemic to Nechisar National Park.
In 2025, researchers taking DNA samples from the Nechisar specimen and other African nightjars concluded that the individual was likely a hybrid of a female Standard-winged Nightjar and a male Freckled Nightjar. This hybrid combination would be a first confirmed case of hybridization of Old World nightjars.


References


External links
BirdLife Species Factsheet.

The '''Nechisar nightjar''' ('''''Caprimulgus solala''''') is a species of [[nightjar]] in the family [[Caprimulgidae]]. It is now thought to be a hybrid. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Ethiopia]].<ref name="iucn status" />

The species was first discovered in 1990 when researchers discovered a decomposing specimen in the [[Nechisar National Park]].<ref name="redlist">{{Cite news|title=Caprimulgus solala|url={{IUCNlink|150445}}|periodical=BirdLife International 2008|series=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species|year=2009|access-date=19 July 2009}}</ref><ref name="NPR">{{cite news|title=A Single Wing Starts Quest For Mystery Bird|last=LeMoult|first=Craig|url=https://www.npr.org/templates/story/story.php?storyId=106749870|work=[[Weekend Edition Sunday]]|publisher=[[NPR]]|date=19 July 2009}}</ref> After bringing back a single wing from the specimen to the [[Natural History Museum, London|Natural History Museum]] in London, it was determined to be a previously unknown species.{{Why|date=April 2026|reason=What were the features deemed unique?}} Its specific name, ''solala'', means "only a wing".<ref name="NPR"/>

Its natural [[habitat]] is subtropical. It is probably endemic to [[Nechisar National Park]].{{cn|date=October 2024}}

In 2025, researchers taking DNA samples from the Nechisar specimen and other African nightjars concluded that the individual was likely a hybrid of a female [[Standard-winged Nightjar]] and a male [[Freckled Nightjar]]. This hybrid combination would be a first confirmed case of hybridization of Old World nightjars.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Izaguirre |first1=Frank |title=Has the Nechisar Nightjar Mystery Been Solved? |date=6 May 2025 |url=https://www.aba.org/has-the-nechisar-nightjar-mystery-been-solved |publisher=ABA |access-date=8 May 2025}}</ref><ref>{{cite bioRxiv |last1=Shannon |last2=van Grouw |last3=Collinson |title=Genetic and morphological analysis shows the Nechisar Nightjar is a hybrid |biorxiv=10.1101/2025.04.08.647728}}</ref>
